OECD Food, Agriculture and Fisheries Papers No. 223 Assessing the impacts of agricultural support policies on the environment: Economic analysis, literature findings and synthesis Jussi Lankoski, Eveline Nales, Hugo Valin https://dx.doi.org/10.1787/808f110c-en This document was approved by the OECD Joint Working Party on Agriculture and the Environment following its 57th Session that took place on 7-8 November 2024 and prepared for publication by the OECD Secretariat. OECD TRADE AND AGRICULTURE DIRECTORATE OECD FOOD, AGRICULTURAL AND FISHERIES PAPER N°223 © OECD 2025 Assessing the Impacts of Agricultural Support Policies on the Environment: Economic Analysis, Literature Findings and Synthesis Jussi Lankoski (OECD) Eveline Nales (Wageningen University & Research) and Hugo Valin (OECD) This report provides a comprehensive synthesis of existing knowledge on the impacts of agricultural producer support across environmental domains – air, soil, climate, water and biodiversity. Findings are used to draw a typology, consistent with the OECD framework for measuring agricultural support, that characterises the level of environmental risk or potential benefit associated with each form of support. The most production-coupled forms of support appear to be the most likely to generate environmentally harmful impacts, although these impacts vary depending on the type of production or input being targeted, and possible environmental conditions attached to the support. In contrast, agri-environmental payments, and more particularly performance – or results-based payments directly tied to environmental outcomes – are the most likely to be environmentally beneficial. Local conditions, trade-offs between environmental dimensions and scales of analysis of the impacts (domestic or global level) need to be considered for a complete determination of the impacts of support instruments. Key words: Microeconomic model, Computable general equilibrium, Subsidies, Environmentally harmful, Environmentally beneficial, Market price support JEL codes: Q12, Q15, Q18, Q53, Q54 Acknowledgements The authors are grateful for all the comments received from delegates of the OECD Joint Working Party on Agriculture and the Environment, which helped improve this report. Thanks also go to OECD colleagues who provided comments and edits at different stages of the report preparation, notably Marion Jansen, Julia Nielson, Guillaume Gruère, Martin von Lampe, Roger Martini, Katia Karousakis, Florian Freund and Marcel Adenäuer. Editorial support was provided by Martina Abderrahmane and Michèle Patterson. Most common approaches to reference choice in impact assessment 12  5 OECD FOOD, AGRICULTURAL AND FISHERIES PAPER N°223 © OECD 2025 Key messages • Agriculture affects the environment through many different channels, positively or negatively, depending on farmers’ production systems and practices and how they interact with the local environment. Agricultural policies, and most notably agricultural support, play an important role in shaping farmers’ behaviour, and therefore influence the sector’s impacts on air, water, soil, climate or biodiversity. • A comprehensive synthesis of existing knowledge on the environmental impacts of various forms of agricultural producer support is used to draw a typology, consistent with the OECD framework for measuring support, that characterises the level of environmental risk or potential benefit of each form of support compared to the absence of that support, with the following main findings: o Most production-coupled forms of support appear to be the most likely to generate environmentally harmful impacts, although these vary depending on the type of production or input being targeted by the support, as environmental footprints can differ greatly, as well as on the possible presence of environmental conditions attached to the support instrument. o Less coupled forms of producer support can generate lower environmental harm. Further reduction of these negative impacts and even some environmental benefits can be achieved if these forms of support are accompanied by production and input restrictions, and strong environmental cross-compliance that are well designed and implemented. Agri-environmental payments, and more particularly performance- or results-based payments directly tied to environmental outcomes, are the most likely to be environmentally beneficial. o Market price support (MPS), which encompasses measures inflating producer prices, is found to have a complex role on the environment. It is among the most potentially harmful at domestic level through the production incentives it generates, in absence of production constraints. However, the impacts on some global environment domains, such as climate or biodiversity, can be ambiguous when considering that MPS removal may, in some contexts, relocate production to less environmentally efficient regions of the world. That said, the impacts of MPS on productivity across regions and, through it, on environmental sustainability are not studied in this paper. • This study also highlights the complex trade-offs between environmental dimensions – greenhouse gas emissions, biodiversity, water quality and resources, air and soil quality – and scales of analysis of the impacts (i.e. at the domestic or global level). • Improved data collection on agricultural policy monitoring could ensure that granular features of producer support programmes relevant to the environment are better captured and reflected in international statistics. • While improving the scope, design and implementation of individual support instruments will help improve the effects of agriculture on the environment, delivering broader sustainability objectives (economic, environmental, social) remains essential. Further reforms of support policies and additional policy measures and investments (e.g. regulations and standards, pricing of externalities, investments in innovation and R&D) will be needed for progress in this area. 6  OECD FOOD, AGRICULTURAL AND FISHERIES PAPER N°223 © OECD 2025 Executive Summary Agricultural policies, which influence farmers’ production decisions, can affect the environment positively or negatively, depending on agricultural production systems, farming practices and how they interact with the local environment. These policies can take different forms, including government support and regulations targeting various part of the agriculture and food systems environment. Governments have called out on several occasions the need to phase out the most environmentally harmful support and to promote environmentally beneficial policies and measures, without compromising other sustainability objectives. Progressing on this reform agenda requires a better identification of the positive or negative impacts of different forms of agricultural support on the environment. This report synthesises current research on the environmental impacts of agricultural support. Combining microeconomic analysis, applied modelling and a literature review, it analyses the different channels of impacts of agricultural support instruments on farmers’ behaviour, and the subsequent impacts of farming practices on the environment – greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, biodiversity, water quality and resources, air and soil quality – compared to the absence of support. The findings are used to develop a typology of the level of risk of producer support measures being environmentally harmful, or the potential for support to be environmentally beneficial. Some types of agricultural support can create incentives for farmers to intensify production, through greater use of inputs, or expansion into new areas, which in turn leads to increased risk of pollution or natural resource depletion, while others may have the opposite effect. The entry-exit margin, i.e. the number of producing farms and area under production, can also be affected, inducing notably land use change. According to microeconomic theory, most production coupled forms of support, such as output-based or input-based support, encourage expansion of production along the intensive, extensive and entry-exit margins altogether, resulting in negative consequences across most environmental domains. Conversely, most decoupled forms of support appear to have more neutral impacts, by only impacting the entry-exit margin of production, and/or positive impacts with environmental cross-compliance. Applied economic modelling, using a computable general equilibrium model, confirms that most coupled forms of support are also more likely to be harmful to the environment, but also suggests that the level of impact depends on the sector supported, production conditions, policy environment, and market responses. In addition, some types of intervention can involve trade-offs among environmental domains, or for specific domains, between local and remote impacts, for instance, through global land saving versus local pollution in case of land-use intensification. Conversely, less coupled support can bring local environmental benefits, but only to a limited extent. Agri-environmental measures are needed for larger benefits to materialise, as well as targeted investment strategies, including in R&D, to bring more effective outcomes and deliver on other sustainability objectives. These findings are then compared with the insights from a literature review of 85 articles covering 607 cases of policy impact assessment on various environmental dimensions. Reviewing these cases highlights the importance of distinguishing what production inputs are incentivised and brings additional insights into the role played by environmental cross-compliance and voluntary agri-environmental payments in determining the final impacts of producer payments on the environment. A review of modelling analyses also shows that most coupled forms of support can result in different impacts depending on whether they are budgetary or not (market price support (MPS) versus payments based on output). In the case of MPS, the potentially harmful impacts of support on the local environment are well characterised, but studies vary on the global impact of these instruments. Some studies have indicated that when these instruments are removed, there is a risk that some environmental outcomes may worsen at global level, notably climate, biodiversity and water resources, in cases where production is relocated to less environmentally efficient regions of the world without any accompanying measures. However, these accompanying measures are not investigated in this report. Removal of MPS may also result, in other cases, in production shifting to more environmentally efficient regions of the world. The long-term impacts of support on productivity growth are also not examined in this study but are an additional element affecting environmental sustainability across regions. Combining the different sets of findings across these approaches enables a better picture of the landscape of potential environmental impacts of various forms of producer support, using the OECD support  7 OECD FOOD, AGRICULTURAL AND FISHERIES PAPER N°223 © OECD 2025 categorisation as a starting point for the analysis. These impacts appear to be strongly influenced by several cross-cutting elements, such as the type of instrument (through its modalities of coupling to production decisions), the type of production or input to which it applies, and the environmental modalities attached to the payment. To determine the final impact of support measures, in many cases, it is also necessary to consider several elements of local context – the state of the environment in the various domains considered, the farming conditions and structure, the broader environmental policy framework, and the degree of interactions with international markets. Based on this framework, the report identifies several forms of support that are potentially most harmful or beneficial for the environment compared to no policy intervention: • Forms of support the most likely to be harmful, or high risk, for the environment are found to be (1) payments based on output, animal number or area, for production with high environmental footprint (e.g. GHG emission-intensive livestock production systems, crop varieties with high water or pesticide requirement); (2) payments to variable input with high damaging potential (e.g. fertiliser, fossil fuel, feed use, or water use); or (3) support to investments into assets worsening environmental impacts (e.g. groundwater irrigation; livestock barns expansion), with each of these measures exempt from specific form of restriction or environmental provisions. • Attaching specific conditions – particularly strong environmental compliance, production and input constraints, and agri-environmental targeting – to these and other less production-coupled support measures can limit their degree of harmfulness and, if well-designed, lead to potential environmental benefits compared to no policy intervention. • Forms of support that are the most likely to deliver tangible environmental benefits are found to be (1) support reducing consumption of inputs with high environmental damage potential or increasing those of inputs with high benefit potential, based on performance- or results-based payment criteria; (2) support to investments into assets with direct environmental benefits (e.g. renewable energy, improved manure management storage); (3) performance or results-based agri-environmental payments; (4) payments for natural resource retirement from production – including environmentally sensitive land; and (5) payments for environmental goods and services on agricultural land. Data exist within international statistics to track some of these payments but not all of them, underscoring the benefits of more information collection to better characterise specific forms of support. The report provides recommendations to improve this data collection, in particular by gathering more detailed information related to voluntary agri-environmental payments, environmental cross-compliance modalities and characteristics of input subsidies. The present characterisation can help assess the level of risk of harmful impact and the potential for environmental benefit associated with the design of agricultural policy, irrespective of other criteria. This could be used to infer the impact of a number of simple instruments (e.g. payments for fossil fuel consumption or waterside buffer strips), while for more complex programmes (e.g. adoption of specific practices), elements of context will remain critical to determine the expected environmental outcomes. Finally, the analysis focuses on the environmental impacts of support measures compared to no policy intervention. Therefore, the potential environmental benefits of specific support instruments do not imply these are the most efficient measures to address the environmental issues at stake. In the case of most market distortive instruments, less distorting measures could be considered to address these environmental objectives. Improving the design of agricultural support requires, however, careful consideration and balancing of the trade-offs between all sustainability objectives (environmental, social, economic) at the domestic and global levels. Considering alternative policy options against a broader set of objectives could be considered for future work. 8  OECD FOOD, AGRICULTURAL AND FISHERIES PAPER N°223 © OECD 2025 1. Introduction Agriculture occupies a third of global land and therefore significantly shapes landscapes, ecosystems, and the global environment, which brings risks and opportunities. The agricultural sector can alter land, water and air quality, has been identified as a driver of wildlife habitat fragmentation and biodiversity loss, and contributes to climate change (Tilman et al., 2002[1]; Steinfeld et al., 2006[2]; Campbell et al., 2017[3]). These negative impacts have been attributed to the more intensive use of inputs (e.g. chemicals and synthetic fertilisers, water for irrigation, or intensive grazing); changes in farmland uses (including a move towards specialisation and the uniformity of landscapes), and land conversion to and from agriculture (Foley et al., 2011[4]; Hardelin and Lankoski, 2018[5]). On the other hand, agriculture can also provide various forms of environmental benefits and ecosystem services, including flood and erosion prevention, carbon sequestration, wildlife habitat conservation, and wild species diversity (Hardelin and Lankoski, 2018[5]). Agricultural policies play an important role in shaping farmers’ activities, practices, and their business environment. Such policies can notably take the form of public support or regulations, targeting various parts of the agriculture and food systems environment. In particular, the agriculture sector received an average USD 842 billion per year of transfers across the OECD and major emerging economies in 2021-2023 (OECD, 2024[6]). The effect of this support on the environment is increasingly discussed in the context of the need for a transition towards more sustainable food systems. A significant share of this support is budgetary (USD 508 billion per year in 2021-23) and corresponds to government expenditures toward producers, consumers and general services for the sector. The rest is transferred in the form of market price support (MPS) (USD 334 billion per year) due to border measures or other forms of price intervention. Individual producers directly receive most agricultural support (USD 629 billion per year of producer support on average in 2021-23), which influence the choice of their production methods and farming practices (ibid.). Many recent studies highlighted that reorienting support towards more environmentally beneficial measures could improve the environmental performance of the sector (FAO, UNDP, UNEP, 2021[7]; Gautam et al., 2022[8]; Valin, Henderson and Lankoski, 2023[9]; UNDP, 2024[10]). In November 2022, OECD Ministers of Agriculture articulated a shared vision for governments on the actions needed to transform agriculture and food systems with a view to i) ensuring food security and nutrition, ii) strengthening sustainability, and iii) ensuring inclusive livelihoods. As a part of this agenda, they committed to “intensify efforts as appropriate to reform or reorient agricultural policy, and in particular to address those support measures that are harmful to the environment, to move towards more sustainable agriculture and food systems” (OECD, 2022[11]). They called on the OECD, through the Committee for Agriculture, to support their efforts by “analysing the effectiveness and efficiency of existing and potential agricultural policies to incentivise improved environmental outcomes from food systems” (§ 28), and by “examining environmentally harmful and beneficial support measures and providing evidence-based analysis to support reform to improve environmental outcomes” (§ 29) (ibid). Most OECD countries also took more specific commitments on environmentally harmful subsidies in the context of the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD). The CBD Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework, adopted in December 2022, calls on parties in its Target 18 to “identify by 2025, and eliminate, phase out or reform incentives, including subsidies, harmful for biodiversity, in a proportionate, just, fair, effective and equitable way, while substantially and progressively reducing them by at least 500 billion United States dollars per year by 2030, starting with the most harmful incentives, and scaling up positive incentives for the conservation and sustainable use of biodiversity.” This objective should however be articulated with other goals of agricultural and food systems policies, as highlighted in the “COP 28 Sustainable agriculture, resilient food systems, and climate action” declaration, signed in December 2023 by 159 countries. These countries committed to “revisit or orient policies and public support related to agriculture and food systems to promote activities which increase incomes, reduce greenhouse gas emissions, and bolster resilience, productivity, livelihoods, nutrition, water efficiency and human, animal and ecosystem health while reducing food loss and waste, and ecosystem loss and degradation.” To support this agenda, a better understanding of the environmental impacts of agricultural support is needed, in complement to evidence on performance along other sustainability policy objectives, such as food security, public health and livelihoods. Attempts to categorise agricultural support according to their environmental impacts are not new (Lewandrowski, Tobey and Cook, 1997[12]; Kondouri et al., 2009[13])  9 OECD FOOD, AGRICULTURAL AND FISHERIES PAPER N°223 © OECD 2025 but require refining. Recent OECD analyses, using the Producer Support Estimate (PSE) classification of agricultural support policies, have highlighted that market price support, producer support based on commodity outputs and variable inputs without constraints are potentially the most environmentally harmful forms of support, whereas fully decoupled payments based on non-current crop area as the least environmentally harmful forms of support (Henderson and Lankoski, 2019[14]; DeBoe, 2020[15]). In contrast, payments that incentivise the use of environmentally friendly inputs or practices could bring environmental benefits if they follow good policy design and implementation principl

Front. Sustain. Food Syst., 04 December 2024

Sec. Agricultural and Food Economics

Volume 8 - 2024 | <https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2024.1474903>

# The economic and environmental sustainability dimensions of agriculture: a trade-off analysis of Italian farms

![Brunella Arru
](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/2393431/74)
![Federica Cisilino](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/2838792/74)
![Paola Sau](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/2870367/74)
![Roberto Furesi](https://loop.frontiersin.org/cdn/images/profile/default_32.jpg)
![Pietro Pulina](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/134650/74)
![Fabio A. Madau,](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/137880/74)

Crop and livestock farms are central to achieving the 2030 Agenda goals and a sustainable agri-food system. However, the transition toward a sustainable agri-food system requires optimizing several economic and environmental farm targets that, interacting with one another, would lead to win-win opportunities, at least as desired by the European Union (EU) policies. Indeed, in recent years, the EU has fostered sustainable development in a logic of synergy between farms’ environmental and economic performances. This work fits into the agricultural sustainability assessment with the aim of improving our understanding of the existence of synergy or a trade-off between the economic and environmental dimensions at a crop and livestock field and farm scale. Specifically, using a set of appropriate agricultural economic and environmental indicators, two composite indexes were created and used to perform trade-off analysis on 7.891 farms that participated in 2019 and 2020 in the Italian Farm Accountancy Data Network. The findings showed a trade-off between economic and environmental dimensions in all livestock sub-sectors and the cereals sector, while a synergy in the horticulture sector. Considering the new European sustainability policies on agriculture and global scenarios, the study significantly contributes to policymakers, practitioners, and academic debate on sustainability in agriculture.

## 1 Introduction

In the face of runaway climate change, the loss of ecosystems and biological species, and rapid growth in inequality, humans face a shift to a sustainable development paradigm that requires changes in their economic system, institutions, and relationships with nature ([Uitto, 2022](#ref117)). Within this framework, agriculture plays a significant role as both an important driver and a significant threat ([Rockström et al., 2017](#ref104); [Velten et al., 2015](#ref119)). Indeed, agriculture significantly contributes to the success of all Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) of Agenda 2030, and concerning ongoing population growth and increasing food demands, it is strategic in food security ([Arru et al., 2022](#ref5); [Fackelmann et al., 2019](#ref52); [Godfray et al., 2010](#ref60); [Kanter et al., 2018](#ref70)). In particular, it is increasingly clear that the food system is linked not only to SDG 2 (aimed at ending hunger and achieving food security and improved nutrition) but also to other development challenges addressed in the SDGs, such as poverty alleviation (SDG 1), education (SDG 4), gender equality (SDG 5), energy use (SDG 7), economic growth and employment (SDG 8), sustainable consumption and production (SDG 12), climate change (SDG 13) and ecosystem management (SDG 15).

Agricultural production encounters several challenges related to sustainable development, which have long-term effects. These include the need to boost agricultural output, the adverse environmental impact of agriculture, but also its substantial role in enhancing human life quality. Those matters have brought attention to the relationship between sustainable development and agricultural production, underscoring the critical role of agricultural production’s economic and environmental sustainability in ensuring the long-term sustainability of food systems and the well-being of current and future generations ([Bi et al., 2024](#ref12); [Kalinowska et al., 2022](#ref69)).

A genuine sustainability transition in agriculture is vital for ensuring global food security, societal well-being, and environmental protection ([Campagnolla et al., 2019](#ref20); [El Bilali, 2020](#ref46)).

This transition involves institutional, political, economic, and socio-cultural dimensions and actors (like farmers called to change several practices), with diverse impacts depending on factors like agro-ecological zone, farming system, cultural preferences, institutions, and policies ([El Bilali, 2019](#ref45); [Kanter et al., 2018](#ref70); [Vermunt et al., 2020](#ref120)). The European Commission aims to set a global standard for sustainable food through various policies, such as the EU Green Deal and the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP; [European Commission, 2020](#ref47)). These policies strive to harmonize sustainability objectives and mitigate trade-offs among different targets.

However, agriculture’s sustainability is rather complex, with potential trade-offs such as biodiversity loss or soil pollution from fertilizers ([Martinho, 2020](#ref83)). Therefore, understanding and managing these trade-offs is critical for effective policy and decision-making ([Godfray et al., 2010](#ref60); [MEA, 2005](#ref85)). To guide this transition, it is essential to develop indices that assess the multifaceted nature of sustainability and evaluate potential outcomes ([Conway and Barbier, 1990](#ref31); [Dumanski et al., 1998](#ref42); [Sidhoum et al., 2022](#ref108)).

Trade-off analysis in agricultural systems, which examines resource scarcity and opportunity costs, is increasingly important for understanding sustainability across different scales—global, regional, and individual farm levels ([Breure et al., 2024](#ref15); [Crissman et al., 1998](#ref35); [Rufino et al., 2011](#ref105); [Stoorvogel et al., 2004](#ref111)). This analysis helps clarify the relationships between economic and environmental dimensions of sustainability, which vary based on stakeholder priorities and spatial scale—from global to local—([Kanter et al., 2018](#ref70); [Rufino et al., 2011](#ref105)). In particular, the individual crop field scale—where the primary goals are to maximize productivity and minimize environmental impacts while also balancing productivity, income, and social objectives at the farm level ([Kanter et al., 2018](#ref70); [Rufino et al., 2011](#ref105))—takes on particular importance: it is considered the most suitable for assessing and implementing sustainability measures ([Kelly et al., 2018](#ref71)), and it is the focal point of most policies ([OECD, 2001](#ref92)).

From these considerations, this study aims to address the following research question: “What is the relationship between the economic and environmental sustainability dimensions in different crop and livestock sectors?”

The paper builds on previous research that either focused on a single sector ([Sau et al., 2023](#ref107)) or explored different regional contexts ([Sidhoum et al., 2022](#ref108)). It investigates Italy, which has demonstrated significant progress and strong capabilities in sustainable agricultural resource management ([Coluccia et al., 2020](#ref30)). Additionally, Italian farms, with their rich cultural, social, and political traditions and positive relationship with the environment and territorial areas, provide a valuable benchmark for examining the primary sector and its current challenges.

To answer this research question, the study analyzes data from 7,891 farms in Italy from 2019 and 2020, sourced from the Italian Farm Accountancy Data Network (FADN), that is a valuable source of information to carry out different analyses and addresses a wide range of policy analysis needs ([Marongiu et al., 2021](#ref81)).

This paper makes several contributions. This research contributes to current academic discussions on agricultural sustainability by providing insights into trade-offs and synergies between economic and environmental dimensions. To our knowledge, it is the first effort to estimate the trade-offs between the economic and environmental dimensions of Italian agriculture using the FADN database. Additionally, it provides crucial insights for both national and international policymakers. Given that trade-off analysis is an effective method for evaluating the complex dynamics of farming systems, offering invaluable insights into their policy impacts, ([Klapwijk et al., 2014](#ref72); [Špička et al., 2020](#ref110)), this paper enhances our understanding of the potential synergy or trade-off between the economic and environmental aspects at both the crop and livestock field and farm scale. It provides quantitative information on competing indicators that can help inform policy and decision-making. While new EU policies aim to foster synergies between sustainability dimensions, our findings indicate that a win-win scenario—where both environmental and economic goals are achieved simultaneously—occurs only in certain types of farms.

The paper is structured as follows: Section 2 outlines the research methodology and sample description, Section 3 presents the results, and Section 4 discusses the findings, concludes the paper, and offers recommendations for practitioners, academics, and policymakers.

## 2 Background

The European Commission (EC) is committed to establishing European food as the global standard for sustainability through several new policies, including the EU Green Deal, the Farm to Fork (F2F) and Biodiversity Strategies, Horizon Europe, Next Generation EU, and the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP). These policies aim to drive a just transition ([European Commission, 2020](#ref47)) by fostering synergies and minimizing trade-offs among various sustainability dimensions and targets embedded in the European strategy. For instance, the CAP 2023–2027 focuses on combating climate change, protecting the environment, and conserving landscapes and biodiversity, while also promoting vibrant rural areas, ensuring fair incomes for farmers, boosting competitiveness, and enhancing farmers’ roles in the food chain. Similarly, the F2F strategy aims to harness the economic benefits of transitioning to more sustainable practices ([Mowlds, 2020](#ref88)), realizing win-win opportunities that improve sustainable development and profits in a logic of synergy between farms’ environmental and economic dimensions.

However, each pathway involves varying sets and/or levels of environmental and socio-economic trade-offs and synergies that must be acknowledged and managed ([Kanter et al., 2018](#ref70)). Positive outcomes can sometimes overshadowed by negative externalities, such as biodiversity loss or soil pollution from fertilizers and crop protection products ([Martinho, 2020](#ref83)). Therefore, it is crucial to examine and manage the trade-offs between the potential benefits and negative impacts resulting from the interaction of food production with other aspects of sustainable agriculture ([Godfray et al., 2010](#ref60); [MEA, 2005](#ref85)).

Previous research on these relationships within the agricultural sustainability paradigm has been inconclusive ([Grzelak et al., 2022](#ref62)). Some studies suggest synergies between sustainability dimensions ([Berre et al., 2014](#ref11); [Bonfiglio et al., 2017](#ref14); [Gómez-Limón and Sanchez-Fernandez, 2010](#ref61); e.g., [Grzelak et al., 2022](#ref62); [Picazo-Tadeo et al., 2011](#ref96)), while others highlight trade-offs (e.g., [Briner et al., 2013](#ref16); [Calzadilla et al., 2010](#ref19); [Eigenbrod et al., 2009](#ref44); [Galdeano-Gómez et al., 2017](#ref54); [Jaklič et al., 2014](#ref68); [Sulewski et al., 2018](#ref112)). According to [Sidhoum et al. (2022)](#ref108), achieving a successful transition requires optimizing interconnected economic, environmental, and social objectives, which can lead to unintended adverse effects in some cases, while trade-offs and synergies may occur in others.

For a successful agricultural sector transition, it is crucial to (i) develop indices that assess the multifaceted nature of sustainability and consider interrelation among its dimensions, thereby providing a concrete and actionable framework for decision-making at all levels ([Conway and Barbier, 1990](#ref31); [Dumanski et al., 1998](#ref42)), (ii) monitor potential outcomes, evaluate whether setting goals are met or threatened, and recommend adjustments as needed ([Kanter et al., 2018](#ref70); [Sidhoum et al., 2022](#ref108)).

In this field, a growing interest in agriculture’s economic and environmental performance has been observed in the theoretical and empirical literature ([Aldieri et al., 2019](#ref4); [Kanter et al., 2018](#ref70); [Sidhoum et al., 2022](#ref108)), along with a demand for farm-level sustainability indices based on a set of indicators ([de Olde et al., 2016](#ref38); [Singh et al., 2012](#ref109); [van Huylenbroeck et al., 2000](#ref118)) that help in understanding the trade-offs between these dimensions to develop effective policies and practices.

In this context, providing a quick overview of the significant findings from recent studies focused on using indicators to measure and assess these trade-offs appears useful. First and foremost, it is evident that several studies have emphasized the need to manage these trade-offs effectively to balance food production with environmental conservation ([Godfray et al., 2010](#ref60)). While some research suggests synergies between sustainability dimensions, others highlight significant trade-offs, particularly in cases involving biodiversity loss or soil pollution from agricultural inputs ([Berre et al., 2014](#ref11); [Briner et al., 2013](#ref16); [Grzelak et al., 2022](#ref62)). To better understand and study these trade-offs, researchers have developed a variety of sustainability indices based on economic and environmental indicators ([Conway and Barbier, 1990](#ref31); [Dumanski et al., 1998](#ref42)). A growing body of literature has focused on identifying and measuring these indicators at the farm level, with studies suggesting a mix of results across different agricultural systems. These works inform policy decisions and offer practical tools to help farmers and policymakers to address the complex trade-offs that arise between environmental sustainability and economic viability.

Summarizing, previous studies tend to differ and relate primarily based on three core themes: (i) the conceptual frameworks for sustainability assessment, (ii) the methodological approaches for indicator development, and (iii) the empirical insights into the trade-offs between environmental and economic performance at the farm level.

Within the first theme, a key similarity among the studies is their shared goal of balancing economic and environmental dimensions within the broader sustainability paradigm. [Callens and Tyteca (1999)](#ref18) proposed a framework for integrating environmental sustainability with productive efficiency at the firm level. They emphasized the need for indicators reflecting economic performance and environmental impact, suggesting that firms must balance these aspects to achieve sustainable development. They provide foundational frameworks for integrating economic and environmental indicators, setting the stage for subsequent research. Their approach is one of the earliest to systematically link these dimensions, making it foundational for subsequent studies. Building on this foundation, [Chopin et al. (2021)](#ref24) conducted an extensive review of sustainability assessment tools, frameworks, and indicator advancements. They highlighted key areas for improvement, emphasizing the necessity for more refined tools and indicators to effectively capture the complexities of farm sustainability. They built on these frameworks by refining tools and indicators, addressing the need for more accurate and context-specific assessments. Indeed, this is a crucial concern for policymakers seeking to harmonize environmental and economic goals in agriculture. This shift reflects an evolving understanding of sustainability, which requires not just the balance between economic and environmental goals but also accounting for the interactions between these dimensions and the socio-economic context of farms. In contrast, [Pacini et al. (2004)](#ref94) and [Medici et al. (2020)](#ref86) contribute to the theoretical discussion by demonstrating the applicability of multi-objective modeling and innovative practices in achieving sustainability goals. [Pacini et al. (2004)](#ref94) employed ecological-economic modeling to support policymaking in Tuscany. Their approach integrates environmental and economic objectives, demonstrating the potential for multi-objective frameworks in agricultural policy. [Medici et al. (2020)](#ref86) analyzed the environmental impacts of fruit cultivation from a business innovation perspective. Their study highlights the role of innovative practices in mitigating environmental effects while maintaining economic viability. Though focused on different regions and sectors, both studies reflect a broader convergence toward multi-objective and context-specific approaches to sustainability.

Concerning the second theme, the studies commonly rely on sustainability indicators, despite the various methodologies employed to develop these indicators. [Callens and Tyteca (1999)](#ref18) advocate for developing indicators that balance productive efficiency and environmental sustainability, setting the stage for subsequent methodological advancements. [Chopin et al. (2021)](#ref24) take a step further by reviewing existing tools and suggesting improvements in the design and application of sustainability indicators. [Latruffe et al. (2016)](#ref76) contribute to the methodological discussion by promoting a multidimensional approach to sustainability assessment, using indicators that span economic, environmental, and social dimensions, providing a foundation for more open evaluations of farm sustainability. The practical application of the multidimensional approach proposed by [Latruffe et al. (2016)](#ref76) and then developed by [Gaviglio et al. (2017)](#ref55) highlighted how tailored indicators can be used to evaluate sustainability in region-specific contexts. The divergence in methodologies is most evident in the empirical studies. The methodological approach of [Baldoni et al. (2017](#ref6), [2018)](#ref7) highlights the urgent and important challenges of using environmental indicators to assess farm performance, revealing a complex relationship between increased productivity and negative environmental externalities. In contrast, [Coppola et al. (2020)](#ref33) rely on income and economic viability indicators drawn from FADN data to evaluate the financial sustainability of Italian farms. These two approaches—one focused on environmental impacts, the other on economic outcomes—describe the methodological trade-offs involved in measuring sustainability across different dimensions.

Finally, it can be observed that while the conceptual frameworks and methodological approaches show convergence in their shared emphasis on sustainability, the empirical findings of the reviewed studies reveal significant differences in how these frameworks are implemented at the farm level, particularly in balancing trade-offs between environmental performance and economic viability. Empirical studies such as those by [Baldoni et al. (2017](#ref6), [2018)](#ref7), [Coppola et al. (2020)](#ref33), and [Cortignani et al. (2021)](#ref34) focus on farm-level interactions between productivity and environmental performance. They highlight the challenges and potential solutions for balancing these dimensions, emphasizing the importance of context-specific approaches. [Baldoni et al. (2017)](#ref6) investigated the relationship between productivity and environmental performance, explicitly examining carbon footprint data from Lombardy farms. Their study reveals the complexities of balancing productivity with environmental impacts. [Baldoni et al. (2018)](#ref7) further explored the intricate relationship between environmental performance and productivity, highlighting the challenges of achieving sustainable outcomes at the farm level. [Coppola et al. (2020)](#ref33) analyzed Italian farms’ income levels and economic viability using FADN data. Their findings highlight factors influencing farm income and sustainability, providing insights into the economic dimensions of farm sustainability. This comprehensive approach is echoed in the work of [Gaviglio et al. (2017)](#ref55), who developed a tool for assessing farm sustainability in Italy. By focusing on the selection and adaptation of context-specific indicators, their tool provides practical insights into how tailored metrics can be used to evaluate farm sustainability by analyzing relevant case studies. Lastly, [Westbury et al. (2011)](#ref122) used FADN data to assess the environmental performance of English arable and livestock farms, offering practical examples of how environmental indicators can inform farm management practices. Their work, along with other empirical studies, illustrates the potential for indicators to guide decision-making at the farm level and inform broader agricultural policies. [Cortignani et al. (2021)](#ref34) further contribute to the empirical debate by examining adaptation strategies in response to climate change. They evaluated adaptation strategies to climate change in major Italian agricultural areas, assessing their impact on farm profitability. Their findings emphasize the need for effective adaptation measures to ensure farm sustainability and profitability. In contrast, [Westbury et al. (2011)](#ref122) present a more optimistic view by demonstrating that environmentally friendly practices can lead to positive economic outcomes, mainly when supported by appropriate policy frameworks. They used FADN data to assess the environmental performance of English arable and livestock farms to provide valuable insights into how environmental indicators relate to farm practices and outcomes. They provide practical examples of how environmental indicators can be used to evaluate farm performance, further informing policy and practice.

The reviewed studies converge in recognizing the importance of balancing environmental and economic sustainability at the farm level. Considering both theoretical and empirical contributions, there is a shared emphasis on the need to refine tools and indicators to capture the multifaceted nature of sustainability. [Callens and Tyteca (1999)](#ref18) and [Chopin et al. (2021)](#ref24) point out the key theoretical groundwork, while the empirical research by [Baldoni et al. (2017)](#ref6), [Coppola et al. (2020)](#ref33), and others provide farm-level insights into how these trade-offs play out in practice. However, differences from the previous literature regarding both methodological and empirical findings highlight the complexities and implications of balancing environmental performance and economic feasibility in different contexts. What generally emerges is firstly the importance of context-specific approaches to sustainability, as trade-offs between environmental performance and economic viability can vary significantly depending on the region, farming system, and policy environment. Secondly, the contributions of these studies are crucial for policymakers as they emphasize the need for tailored strategies that balance the diverse goals of environmental protection, economic growth, and social well-being. They offer valuable insights into the trade-offs and potential solutions to achieve sustainability. As the discussion around sustainable farming continues, our paper aims to contribute to the debate by refining assessment tools, further exploring practices at the farm level (using indicators), and addressing the challenges identified in previous studies to advance sustainable agricultural practices.

## 3 Data and method

### 3.1 Data source

Data were gathered from the Italian FADN, a database widely used in the research of sustainability in agriculture ([Bazzani et al., 2021](#ref9); [Boggia et al., 2022](#ref13); [Cardillo et al., 2023](#ref21); [Coppola et al., 2022](#ref32); [Dabkiene et al., 2021](#ref36); [Liberati et al., 2022](#ref78)) as monitors farms’ income, cost, and agricultural holdings activities, beyond to offer pivotal information concerning the impact of the measures taken under public policies. In the Italian context, several studies have employed the FADN both to assess the impact of rural development policies at a micro-level ([Cagliero et al., 2021](#ref17); [Cisilino et al., 2023](#ref27); [Cisilino et al., 2021](#ref29)) and to explore environmental issues with a focus on organic farming ([Cisilino et al., 2019](#ref25); [Cisilino and Cesaro, 2009](#ref26); [Cisilino and Vanni, 2019](#ref28)).

Based on the desire to avoid conjectures based on specific years, the sample investigated includes all Italian agricultural farms that fall in both 2019 and 2020 (the last data years available) into two macro-sectors: crop farms (field crops, except cereals; cereals; horticulture; grape; olive; orchards; fruits) and livestock farms (sheep, goats-herbivore; milk; granivores). In this research, farming systems that involve both crop and livestock production (mixed farming) were not included in the sample since the FADN data do not allow precise and distinct identification of the environmental and economic performances of the two types of activities and a correct calculation of the relevant individual indicators. The sample included 7,927 farms (2,488 livestock farms and 5,439 crop farms). Moreover, because the outliers’ values can distort statistical analyses and violate their assumptions, we removed them from the sample data using the Tukey Fence method ([Tukey, 1977](#ref115)), a popular simple outlier detector for one-dimensional number arrays. It is calculated by creating a “fence” boundary with a 1.5 inter quartile range (IQR) distance beyond the 1st and 3rd quartiles. Any data beyond these fences are outliers. The check for outliers was performed by examining the economic dimension and precisely the net income indicator documented by the Italian FADN for each farm. Consequently, our total constant sample was reduced to 7,891 farms.

### 3.2 Research model

The research was conducted in four steps (see [Figure 1](#fig1)).

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1474903/fsufs-08-1474903-HTML/image_m/fsufs-08-1474903-g001.jpg)

**Figure 1**. Research model.

As a first step, it was necessary to select indicators, which is a complex and decisive process ([Gebara et al., 2024](#ref56)), as they must provide reliable information and be aligned and relevant to the research context and goals.

Afterward, all indicators were calculated for each farm, according to the data reported in the FADN.

The third step aims to evaluate the existence and extent of synergies or compromises between the two dimensions of sustainability. To this end, the following procedural steps were envisaged: (a) ranking of the indicators of each farm 0 to 1; (b) normalization of each indicator to reduce the different measurement scales to a standardized one; (c) develop composite economic and environmental indicators; (d) correlation analysis between composite indicators.

The last step concerns the analysis of the relationships between the individual indicators for each subsector, which will provide more information for understanding where the trade-off (or synergy) unfolds most.

### 3.3 Variables used in the analysis

Agricultural trade-off analysis relies on indicators ([Giller et al., 2014](#ref58)). They must provide significant information to facilitate assessment and decision-making by farmers and policymakers beyond to increase farmers’ awareness or understanding of the potentially important issues being monitored ([Kanter et al., 2018](#ref70); [Pannell and Glenn, 2000](#ref95)).

The selection of the indicators (Step 1 of [Figure 1](#fig1)) considered the following criteria ([Dale and Beyeler, 2001](#ref37); [Pannell and Glenn, 2000](#ref95)): well-understood, unambiguous, and sensitive links between the indicator and what it represents; reliability and accuracy of brought information; sensitivity to natural and anthropogenic stresses to the system; capability of anticipating and preventing impending changes, as well as predicting changes that could be prevented through effective management.

Indicators in the FADN database were used to evaluate sustainability dimensions with specificities inherent to the sector type. Specifically, data from FADN integrated with Italian FADN was used. The latter provides more information and environmental and social variables relating to company management that exceed those required by community legislation, allowing national agricultural peculiarities to be considered ([Turchetti et al., 2022](#ref116)).

It is important to highlight that indices based on monetary values can be influenced by price trends rather than the more or less efficient use or impact of input factors. This could cause performance asymmetry between farms. However, this effect is significantly mitigated by the fact that the survey takes 2 years and the high number of companies included in the sample.

Six economic indicators already applied in previous research ([Bereżnicka, 2018](#ref10); [Coppola et al., 2022](#ref32); [Díaz de Otálora et al., 2021](#ref40); [Masi et al., 2021](#ref84); [Ripoll-Bosch et al., 2012](#ref1

enriched_points.point_title: Integration of Economic and Environmental Goals
enriched_points.expanded_content: Policies must harmonize economic growth with environmental sustainability to achieve a balanced agricultural system that supports both productivity and ecological health.
Providing assistance to vulnerable farming communities enables equitable access to resources, fostering resilience and promoting sustainable practices tailored to local needs.
Adapting policies to local contexts and changing conditions ensures effective implementation, allowing farmers to adopt sustainable practices without economic burdens.
Engaging farmers, governments, NGOs, and researchers fosters shared knowledge and resources, enhancing the effectiveness of policies aimed at sustainable agricultural practices.
